one_liner: A 10-month-old boy born at home without newborn screening is brought in for developmental delay; his mother notes unusually fair skin and a 'mousy, musty' body odor.

stage2.hpi_extension: The parents are recent immigrants and the baby was born at home and never had newborn screening. They report he is not sitting unsupported, has no babbling, and has had two episodes of generalized seizures over the past month. On close questioning, his mother mentions a peculiar 'musty' or 'mousy' body odor and has noted that he is fairer-skinned and has lighter hair than either parent or his older sibling. He has also developed a scaly eczematous rash.
stage2.pmh: Home birth; no newborn screening; no prior hospitalizations
stage2.family_history: Parents are first cousins (consanguineous); one paternal uncle reportedly had intellectual disability and died young
stage2.social_history: Lives with parents and 3-year-old sister (healthy); breast and formula fed; no aspartame exposure
stage2.physical_exam: General: small, developmentally delayed infant. HEENT: very fair skin, blond hair, and blue eyes (lighter than parents); distinctive musty body odor. Skin: eczematous rash on the cheeks and extensor surfaces. Neuro: global developmental delay, mild hypertonia, hyperreflexia; microcephaly. Cardiovascular and abdominal exams are unremarkable.

lab_results.result: Serum phenylalanine: 24 mg/dL (normal <2; classical PKU >20). Tyrosine: low. Ammonia: normal. Glucose, electrolytes, liver enzymes: normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated phenylalanine with low tyrosine is diagnostic of phenylketonuria. Accumulated phenylalanine is neurotoxic and causes progressive intellectual disability; low tyrosine leads to decreased melanin (fair skin/hair) and decreased catecholamines.

lab_results.result: PAH gene sequencing: homozygous pathogenic mutation in phenylalanine hydroxylase. BH4 (tetrahydrobiopterin) cofactor levels normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Loss-of-function mutations in PAH confirm classical (autosomal recessive) phenylketonuria. Normal BH4 excludes less common cofactor-deficient variants.

lab_results.result: Urine organic acids: elevated phenylpyruvate, phenylacetate, and phenyllactate (the phenylketones responsible for the musty odor).
lab_results.clinical_significance: Accumulated phenylalanine is shunted to phenylketones excreted in the urine, producing the characteristic musty/mousy odor and giving the disease its name.

stage5.final_clue: He is placed on a strict phenylalanine-restricted diet with tyrosine supplementation and advised to completely avoid aspartame-containing foods; his mother is counseled that if his future sisters are affected, strict dietary control during any pregnancy is essential to prevent maternal PKU syndrome in the offspring.

debrief.classic_presentation: Classical PKU presents in infancy (often picked up by newborn screening before symptoms develop) with intellectual disability, seizures, microcephaly, eczema, fair skin and hair (from decreased tyrosine/melanin), and a distinctive musty or 'mousy' body odor. Untreated children develop severe, irreversible intellectual disability. If newborn screening is not performed, symptoms emerge in the first year of life.
debrief.pathophysiology: PKU is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH), the enzyme that converts phenylalanine to tyrosine using tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) as a cofactor. Less commonly, the defect lies in BH4 synthesis or recycling. Phenylalanine accumulates and is shunted to phenylketones (phenylpyruvate, phenyllactate, phenylacetate), which spill into the urine and produce the musty odor. Excess phenylalanine is directly neurotoxic and also competitively inhibits amino acid transport into the brain. Decreased tyrosine reduces melanin and catecholamine production.
debrief.treatment_overview: Lifelong dietary restriction of phenylalanine (avoid high-protein foods and especially aspartame) with tyrosine supplementation. Early and strict dietary control (ideally begun in the first weeks of life after positive newborn screening) prevents intellectual disability. Sapropterin (synthetic BH4) may be useful in selected patients. Women with PKU must maintain tight dietary control before and during pregnancy to prevent maternal PKU syndrome (microcephaly, congenital heart disease, intellectual disability in the unaffected fetus).
high_yield_pearls: PKU = autosomal recessive deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH); requires BH4 as a cofactor.
Classic tetrad: intellectual disability, seizures, fair skin/hair, and a musty body odor.
Newborn screening (Guthrie test) detects PKU by elevated serum phenylalanine.
Treatment is dietary phenylalanine restriction with tyrosine supplementation; avoid aspartame (which contains phenylalanine).
Maternal PKU syndrome: an uncontrolled PKU mother causes microcephaly, intellectual disability, and congenital heart defects in her baby regardless of fetal genotype.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Biochemistry chapter, 'Amino acid disorders — Phenylketonuria' section.
